cards.text: {{c1::Scope creep}} occurs when a project's capabilities grow beyond what was originally intended at the start of the project or project phase.
{{c1::Software verification}} is the process of testing whether or not the software works as expected in all cases.
{{c1::Unit test frameworks}} can help you organize your unit tests by providing consistent structure for writing, maintaining, and executing tests.
{{c1::Integration testing}} tests the integration of a bunch of units together to ensure they work properly when combined.
A {{c1::buffer}} is a piece of memory set aside for storing data temporarily while it is moved from one place to another.
{{c1::std::cerr}} is an output stream (like std::cout) designed to be used for error messages.

cards.front: When should you use assertions vs error handling?
cards.back: Assertions should be used to document cases that should be logically impossible (programming errors). Error handling should be used to handle cases that are possible (runtime errors).

cards.text: In C++, runtime assertions are typically implemented using the {{c1::assert}} preprocessor macro. Assertions are usually turned off in {{c2::non-debug}} code.
A {{c1::static_assert}} is an assertion that is evaluated at {{c2::compile-time}}.

cards.explanation:
Chapter 9 covers error detection and handling:
  • Testing strategies: unit testing, integration testing, code coverage (statement, branch, loop)
  • Common semantic errors: conditional logic, infinite loops, off-by-one, operator precedence, floating point precision, integer division, null statements, assignment vs equality
  • Error handling strategies: handle within function, pass to caller, halt program, throw exception
  • Input validation: robust programs validate input, handle extraction failures, clear error states
  • Assertions: preconditions, invariants, postconditions; assert for programming errors, error handling for runtime errors
